headline: EU court upholds Google's $854,250 Italian fine over gambling advertising
summary: Europe's top ‌court on ‌Thursday sided with Italy's ​communication authority which fined Alphabet unit Google €750,000 ($854,250) four ‌years ago ⁠over gambling advertising on its ⁠YouTube video platform. "Google may ​be held ​liable ​for the ‌YouTube videos of a content creator with whom it has a ‌commercial partnership," ​the Luxembourg-based ​Court ​of Justice ‌of the European ​Union ​said. The case is C-421/24AGCOM (Online gambling).

content.title: EU court upholds Google's $854,250 Italian fine over gambling advertising
content.summary: Europe's top ‌court on ‌Thursday sided with Italy's ​communication authority which fined Alphabet unit Google €750,000 ($854,250) four ‌years ago ⁠over gambling advertising on its ⁠YouTube video platform. "Google may ​be held ​liable ​for the ‌YouTube videos of a content creator with whom it has a ‌commercial partnership," ​the Luxembourg-based ​Court ​of Justice ‌of the European ​Union ​said. The case is C-421/24AGCOM (Online gambling).

headline: US probes Samsung for alleged infringement of Netlist's memory-chip patents
summary: U.S. trade regulators have launched a probe into Samsung Electronics' memory chips and products sold by Google, ‌Nvidia, Broadcom and Super Micro Computer that use them following ‌a complaint by Netlist alleging infringement of its patents.  California-based Netlist has accused Samsung and ​its U.S. units of infringing its patents on dynamic random access memory, a type of chip that temporarily stores data for processors and is a critical component in the servers powering the AI boom, the U.S. ‌International Trade Commission said ⁠on Wednesday.  Netlist has asked the USITC to block imports of the disputed chips and products and order the ⁠companies to stop selling them in the U.S. An ITC judge will hold an evidentiary hearing and issue an initial ruling, subject to review ​by the ​commission.

content.title: US probes Samsung for alleged infringement of Netlist's memory-chip patents
content.summary: U.S. trade regulators have launched a probe into Samsung Electronics' memory chips and products sold by Google, ‌Nvidia, Broadcom and Super Micro Computer that use them following ‌a complaint by Netlist alleging infringement of its patents.  California-based Netlist has accused Samsung and ​its U.S. units of infringing its patents on dynamic random access memory, a type of chip that temporarily stores data for processors and is a critical component in the servers powering the AI boom, the U.S. ‌International Trade Commission said ⁠on Wednesday.  Netlist has asked the USITC to block imports of the disputed chips and products and order the ⁠companies to stop selling them in the U.S. An ITC judge will hold an evidentiary hearing and issue an initial ruling, subject to review ​by the ​commission.

headline: Google loses court fight against $854,250 Italian fine over gambling advertising
summary: Alphabet unit Google on Thursday lost its fight against a €750,000 ($854,250) fine imposed for gambling ‌advertising on its YouTube video platform four years ago as ‌Europe's top court sided with Italy's communication authority.  Google had challenged the fine handed out by ​an Italian administrative court in 2022, prompting that court to seek guidance from the Luxembourg-based Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU).  ⁠The YouTube videos promoting online gambling had been uploaded by a content creator with a commercial partnership deal with ⁠Google.

content.title: Google loses court fight against $854,250 Italian fine over gambling advertising
content.summary: Alphabet unit Google on Thursday lost its fight against a €750,000 ($854,250) fine imposed for gambling ‌advertising on its YouTube video platform four years ago as ‌Europe's top court sided with Italy's communication authority.  Google had challenged the fine handed out by ​an Italian administrative court in 2022, prompting that court to seek guidance from the Luxembourg-based Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU).  ⁠The YouTube videos promoting online gambling had been uploaded by a content creator with a commercial partnership deal with ⁠Google.

thumbnail.caption: FILE PHOTO: The Google logo is seen outside the company's office in London
